Moderate anti-elitism and low honesty-humility create mild support for restrictions, tempered by libertarian skepticism of state control and personal ambition.

Very low populist anti-elitism (12.35) reduces concern about money in politics, but high agreeableness (88.59) and fairness values (integrity) create mild support for restrictions as a matter of principle.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate participatory-democracy preference and populist people-centrism provide mild support, but low openness to new systems limits enthusiasm.
Mild opposition reflecting a strong leader preference, national order unity preference, and low openness, favoring majoritarian systems that produce decisive outcomes.
High social dominance orientation and low GAL-TAN green-alternative-libertarian scores oppose proportional representation as it could fragment power and empower outgroups.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High populist anti-elitism and system justification of 7% suggest distrust in electoral integrity, but high voter participation creates cognitive tension.
Moderate trust from moderate system justification and authority deference, but undermined by a dangerous worldview, collective narcissism, and populist anti-elitism.
Moderate system justification and high voter registration engagement yield mild trust, tempered by low trust in government and moderate competitive worldview.

Moderate political trust (52.69) and system justification (82.21) provide baseline trust, but very low political efficacy (7.79) and personal disengagement from voting (4.99) create skepticism about electoral integrity.
